postText:
50 years of electricity decarbonisation, 1975–2025,
for the largest four European economies,
Germany, UK, France and Italy.

France rather sticks out as the main success story.

⸻
Ref: Low Carbon Power lowcarbonpower.org/chart
keyFindings: France achieved a dramatic reduction in electricity carbon intensity between 1979 and 1987, falling from nearly 500 to 100 gCO2eq/kWh, and currently maintains the lowest level at approximately 40 gCO2eq/kWh.
The United Kingdom's carbon intensity has plummeted since 2012, falling from over 450 gCO2eq/kWh to roughly 230 gCO2eq/kWh by 2023.
Germany started with the highest carbon intensity in 1975 at approximately 660 gCO2eq/kWh and has reduced that figure to around 300 gCO2eq/kWh by 2023.
Italy's carbon intensity remained relatively high and stable between 400-500 gCO2eq/kWh for decades before trending downward to approximately 320 gCO2eq/kWh after 2010.
By 2023, the United Kingdom has overtaken Germany and Italy to become the country with the second-lowest carbon intensity of electricity in this group.
